coredata.dc:title: Chemical-hydrothermal synthesis of oval-shaped graphene/ZnO quantum hybrids and their photocatalytic performances
coredata.dc:creator: Van Thuan, Doan
Nguyen, Tri Khoa
Kim, Soon-Wook
Chung, Jin Suk
Hur, Seung Hyun
Kim, Eui Jung
Hahn, Sung Hong
Wang, Mingsong
coredata.dc:description: Oval-shaped graphene/ZnO quantum hybrid (GZQH) is synthesized via chemical-hydrothermal method and tested for the photoenhanced selective reduction of nitroarenes. A facile molecular fusion process is employed to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from pyrene, which is followed by hydrothermal treatment with embryonic ZnO quantum dots (5nm in size) to yield the GZQHs. Zn2+ ions on ZnO embryo react with a functional group of graphene, which forms Zn-O-C bonding leading to highly crystalline quantum hybrids with uniform interface. The GZQHs have a quenched photoluminescence intensity as compared to the GQDs (2nm in size) due to electron transfer at the graphene-ZnO interface. Hydrogen molecules dissociate into hydrogen atoms by photogenerated electrons which transfer and perturb at the interface under UV irradiation. The GZQHs exhibit an excellent UV-induced catalytic performance for the selective reduction of nitroarenes. The effect of ZnO:graphene ratio on reduction reaction rate constant is also investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Zinc oxide
Quantum hybrid
Chemical-hydrothermal synthesis
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Formation of graphene from pyrene and 1,3,6-trinitropyrene and chemical bonding between the functional group of GQDs and Zn2+ of ZnO QDs to form GZQHs.
(a) HRTEM image of graphene/ZnO quantum dot. Inset: Zigzag structure of mono layer GQDs and the ZnO petal in the [002] direction. (b) TEM EDS mapping of C, Zn and O element distributions in GZQHs.

Schematic illustration of the reduction of o-nitroaniline over a GZQH catalyst. Photogenerated electrons transfer and perturb at ZnO-graphene interface under UV irradiation, enhancing the hydrogenation of o-nitroaniline. The activated hydrogen atoms on the surface of GQDs selectively reduce the NO2 group of o-nitroaniline.

UV–vis spectra of o-nitroaniline reduced over GZQHs at various reaction times (a) in the dark and (b) under UV illumination. (c) A plot of ln (Ct/Co) vs reaction time at different reduction conditions.

head.ce:title: Chemical-hydrothermal synthesis of oval-shaped graphene/ZnO quantum hybrids and their photocatalytic performances

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Leibniz Institute for Catalysis, 18059 Rostock, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Energy Harvest-storage Research Center, University of Ulsan, Ulsan 680-749, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Energy Harvest-storage Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Ulsan 680-749, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oval-shaped graphene/ZnO quantum hybrid (GZQH) is synthesized via chemical-hydrothermal method and tested for the photoenhanced selective reduction of nitroarenes. A facile molecular fusion process is employed to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from pyrene, which is followed by hydrothermal treatment with embryonic ZnO quantum dots (5nm in size) to yield the GZQHs. Zn2+ ions on ZnO embryo react with a functional group of graphene, which forms Zn-O-C bonding leading to highly crystalline quantum hybrids with uniform interface. The GZQHs have a quenched photoluminescence intensity as compared to the GQDs (2nm in size) due to electron transfer at the graphene-ZnO interface. Hydrogen molecules dissociate into hydrogen atoms by photogenerated electrons which transfer and perturb at the interface under UV irradiation. The GZQHs exhibit an excellent UV-induced catalytic performance for the selective reduction of nitroarenes. The effect of ZnO:graphene ratio on reduction reaction rate constant is also investigated.

ce:section.ce:para: Catalytic hydrogenations have been widely applied today in fundamental chemical transformations. The dissociation of hydrogen molecule into atoms, an essential step in the hydrogenation process, usually requires an expensive noble metal catalyst such as Pt, Pd or Au. A lot of effort has been devoted to the approach of a high performance and low cost catalyst for catalytic hydrogenation. One approach is to exploit the advantages of noble metal in the form of nanostructures [1]. Another effective strategy is to introduce a small amount of precious metal into other common elements [2]. For a high-performance and cost-effective hydrogenation process, it is essential to develop new generation non-noble-metal catalysts. Up to date, a few non-noble-metal catalysts for hydrogenation reactions have been developed such as potassium tert-butoxide, organocatalyst, phosphine-borane species and a stable digermane [3–6]. However, these catalysts require severe reaction conditions or do not employ H2 directly. An efficient nonmetal catalyst which reacts cleanly with H2 at mild experimental conditions has not been yet reported.

Graphene is a new generation material which is potentially employed in hydrogenation reactions. Theoretically, graphene is a perfect atomic monolayer with an infinite 2D plane [7]. The imbrication of valance and conduction bands of graphene results in zero-bandgap-semiconductor with a very high electron mobility at room temperature and charge carrier of zero mass [8]. Acting like metal makes graphene become a good candidate for replacing metal in hydrogenation catalysis. Zero dimensional (0D) graphene quantum dots (GQDs) owns their unusual and interesting optical and electrical properties [9,10]. The main disadvantage of colloidal semiconductor quantum dots is their toxicity and coupling difficulty. The GQDs are non-toxic and have desirable electro-optic properties [11,12]. ZnO is widely known as a supporter to improve the performance of a catalyst [13–15]. The ZnO plays two roles in the enhancement process: a physical spacer to increase the surface area and synergetic interaction with electrons at interface. In recent years, graphene/ZnO hybrid nanocomposites have been applied to various devices such as solar cells, gas sensors, fuel cells and ultracapacitors [16–19]. However, the application of graphene/ZnO quantum hybrids to the field that requires clean and non-toxic technology like catalytic hydrogenation is scarcely reported.

Here, we report oval-shaped quantum hybrid structures composed of graphene and ZnO using a facile chemical-hydrothermal method. Electrons photogenerated in the ZnO are transported efficiently into the graphene and have a strong activity at the graphene surface, which can participate in the hydrogenation reaction. Strong oscillation bridges between the ZnO and graphene result in ultrafast photogenerated electrons for enhanced photocatalytic activity. Besides, sensitive emission of both GQDs and ZnO under near UV irradiation leads to an excellent catalytic performance for the reduction of nitroarenes.

25mL of 0.1M zinc acetate in ethanol was mixed with 50mL of 1M NaOH in ethanol under magnetic stirring for 60min. Then, 0.25mL of 0.2M tetraethyl orthosilicate in ethanol was added to the mixture. Precipitated ZnO quantum dots were redispersed in 20mL of ethanol and stored at 0°C.

GQDs were synthesized via a chemical-hydrothermal method reported by Wang et al. [8]. Briefly, 2g of pyrene was added to 160mL of HNO3 and then stirred at 80°C for 12h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with 1L of water, and filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to obtain the produced trinitropyrene. 4g of trinitropyrene and 2.4g of NaOH was dissolved in 300mL of water, ultrasonicated (500W, 40kHz) for 2h, transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for 10h to yield GQDs. The GQD solution was filtered through the membrane and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) for 2days to remove sodium salt and unfused small molecules. The purified black GQDs were dried at 95°C.

0.36g of ZnO quantum dot was dissolved with 300m L of DI water in the presence of NaOH (2.4g) and trinitropyrene (4g). After treated ultrasonically and hydrothermally, the solution was filtered and dialyzed to obtain white-grey graphene/ZnO quantum hybrids (GZQHs) following the GQD synthesis procedure described above. The purified GZQHs were repeatedly washed with ethanol by centrifugation, and finally with water. The washed GZQH powders were dispersed in 10mL of water for further studies.

The morphological properties of the prepared GZQHs were measured by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2200 fs) with an electron gun of 200kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were taken on a SPM-9600 AFM. UV–vis spectroscopy (HP8453) analysis was performed in the wavelength range of 190–1100nm with a 1nm slit width. Photoluminescence spectra were taken at room temperature at a 325nm excitation wavelength of a He-Cd laser (model IK3301R-G, Kimmon Koha) with a 30mW output power.

The catalytic performance of the synthesized GZQHs was evaluated for the reduction of 2-nitroaniline (C6H6N2O2) which is known as a common organic pollutant in industrial wastewater. Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was used as a hydrogen source. 1mL of GZQH solution was mixed with 1.0mL of 0.22M NaBH4 and then refluxed for 1min at room temperature. After that, 10mL of 7.5×10−4 M o-nitroaniline was quickly added to the mixture with stirring to initiate the reduction process. The experiment was carried out under UV irradiation. The color change of the 2-nitroaniline solution during reaction was monitored by measuring the absorbance at 410nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (HP8453). The GZQHs were also tested for the reduction of other nitroarenes including 3-nitrophenol, 4-nitrophenol and 2,4-dinitroaniline.

Fig. 1 describes the synthesis process of GZQHs. The hydrothermal fusion of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon molecules into single-crystalline GQDs reported here occurs at remarkably low temperatures (90–200°C). This low-temperature fusion is realized in alkaline aqueous solutions where the presence of alkaline species enables the elimination of hydrogen, condensation or graphitization and edge functionalization. Before the alkaline hydrothermal treatment, pyrene is required to be nitrated into 1,3,6-trinitropyrene in hot HNO3 [20,21]. The enhanced reaction activity of trinitropyrene is related to three positively charged sites of NO2 groups which undergo a nucleophilic reaction with other alkaline such as OH-, NH3 or NH2NH2. The addition of these alkaline species leads to water-soluble OH- and amine-functionalized GQDs [8]. In the synthesis process of GZQHs, Zn2+ ions at the outermost edges of the embryonic ZnO quantum dots (denoted as Zn2+) react with functional groups in the trinitropyrene, resulting in a local formation of ZnOC bonding [22,23]. In addition, the active 1,3,6-trinitropyrene with three positively charged sites tends to fuse into single-crystalline GQDs in base-assisting hydrothermal media. As ZnOC boding and GQDs are formed, the oxygen functionalities of trinitropyrene are significantly reduced. As a result, monolayer graphene linked to ZnO with a ZnOOC bridge is obtained.

The AFM image and height profile of the GZQH in Fig. S1 reveal that the size of the oval-shaped quantum dot is 7nm which corresponds to the summation of ZnO QD size (5nm, Fig. S2) and GQD size (2nm, Fig. S3). A 2nm shoulder in the height profile is ascribed to the GQD linked to the ZnO QD, indicating that several GQDs are attached to ZnO QDs to form the GZQHs.

The plan-view HRTEM image of the GZQH is shown in Fig. 2 . The GQDs produced from pyrene have clear edges (Fig. 2a). The HRTEM (right upper inset) image of the GQDs shows a lattice fringe of 0.21nm, confirming a high crystallinity of graphene [24]. Because of their small size, the edge structure (e.g., zigzag and armchair edges) of the GQDs is clearly seen in the fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns (highlighted in the inset of Fig. 2a). HRTEM images and their FFT patterns further confirm the hexagonal honeycomb structure and bond length (1.42Å of C–C) of graphene [25]. The HRTEM (right lower inset) image clearly shows that the ZnO QDs are single crystalline. The enlarged view of the HRTEM image reveals that an interplanar spacing in the crystalline petal is 0.26nm, which corresponds to a distance between two (002) planes of the hexagonal wurtzite ZnO, indicating a preferential growth in the [002] direction (c axis) [26]. A close inspection of the dark regions in the HRTEM image taken for the layer with the ZnO QDs reveals that the layer is monolayer graphene because a hexagonal atomic lattice with uniform contrast can be clearly identified and a C-C distance of ~0.14nm agrees well with the theoretical value of graphene. The TEM EDS mapping element distributions in Fig. 2b demonstrate the formation of oval-shaped quantum dots in the large scale.

The observed peculiar edges and shapes of the quantum hybrids are responsible for their unique optical property and electronic structure described below. The GQDs typically exhibit a strong absorption bands in the UV region and an edge extending into the visible range. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs, a wide peak at around 300nm is assigned to the shifted integration of π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds and n-π* transition of CO bonds [27,28]. In the case of GZQHs, the formation of ZnOC bonding results in a strong absorption peak around 350nm which looks like the shape of metal-like plasmon absorption peak. This absorption spectrum is attributed to a high perturbation of transferred electrons at the surface of GZQH (Fig. S4a). The phenomena are also observed in the PL spectra at room temperature. The PL peaks with a very strong intensity appear at 375nm and 550nm for pure ZnO QDs and at 420nm for GQDs, which agree with the reported values at room temperature [29]. For the GZQHs, two emission peaks appear at 430nm and 520nm with a much lower intensity, as a result of quenched recombination in quantum hybrid (Fig. S4b).

Fig. S5 shows the excitation-dependent PL spectra of GQDs and GZQHs. The GQDs prepared from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon are found to be sensitive to the exciton wavelength. As the excitation wavelength is changed from 335 to 585nm, the emission peak is shifted from 540 to 568nm (Fig. S5a). The emission intensity is the highest at 485nm which is in the near UV region. In the case of GZQHs, the emission peak is shifted from 495 to 570nm as the excitation wavelength is changed from 335 to 585nm (Fig. S5b). The PL intensity of GQDs is much higher than that of GZQHs indicating that charge carrier recombination is significantly decreased in graphene/ZnO hybrid because photogenerated electrons transfer from ZnO to graphene. A higher PL intensity of GQDs compared with GZQHs implies that less photogenerated electrons recombine with holes in the GZQHs, resulting in a high electron perturbation at graphene-ZnO interface.

A schematic illustration of the reduction of o-nitroaniline to 1,2 benzenediamine over GZQH catalyst is shown in Fig. 3 . The electron-induced dissociation of hydrogen takes place at the graphene-ZnO interface under UV irradiation. Hydrogen molecules get close to the GZQH surface by van der Waals attractive forces and they are then chemisorbed to dissociate in the electron region of the interface [30]. Under equilibrium conditions, a very small amount of hydrogen molecules will be adsorbed on the GZQH surface and then dissociate. Under UV irradiation, electrons photogenerated at the ZnO surface interact with transferred electrons. These electrons will spread more than equilibrium electron distribution. The electron region of the GZQH interface is enlarged and induces more hydrogen dissociation. The expansion of electron perturbation promotes the chemisorption and dissociation of hydrogen molecules on the GZQH surface. Moreover, the quantum-sized graphene dots contain a large number of functional groups such as OH and COH at their surface. These functional groups serve as physical traps, which catch the dissociated hydrogen atoms and provide a site for spillover. This effect facilitates a reaction between o-nitroaniline and hydrogen atom. Chemical reactions involved in the reduction of o-nitroaniline are as follows [31,32]:
(1)
NaBH 4
+ 4
H 2
O →
NaBO 4
+ 6
H 2
(2)
NO 2
C 6
H 4
NH 2
+ 6 H →
NH 2
C 6
H 4
NH 2
+ 2
H 2
O

Hydrogen molecules generated from NaBH4 source dissociate into H atoms to reduce the o-nitroaniline at the graphene/ZnO surface. The UV irradiation enhances the hydrogen dissociation at the GZQH surface resulting in improved catalytic performance.

The synthesized GZQHs are tested for the catalytic reduction of o-nitroaniline that is known as a harmful and hazardous industrial, agricultural product. Fig. 4 shows the catalytic performance of GZQHs under UV irradiation. The absorption spectra of o-nitroaniline at various reaction times for GZQHs are shown in Fig. 4a (in the dark) and Fig. 4b (UV irradiation). After NaBH4 is introduced, a decrease in the intensity of absorption peak at 410nm and a shift of another peak from 280nm to 290nm are observed with reaction time indicating a gradual reduction of o-nitroaniline to 1,2-benzenediamine [33]. This selective reduction can also be appreciated by a gradual change of the originally yellow solution into a colorless one. The o-nitroaniline is found to be completely reduced after 35min under UV irradiation. If the reduction reaction is assumed to follow first-order kinetics, the reaction rate constant, k, is increased from 0.0253 (in the dark) to 0.1002min−1 (UV irradiation) as shown in Fig. 4c. This result confirms that the photogeneration and transfer of electrons under UV irradiation enhances the catalytic activity of GZQHs. Interestingly, the catalytic efficiency of GZQHs is lower than that of GQDs in the dark, but much higher than that of GQDs under UV irradiation. This can be explained by the fact that some active sites are covered when oval-shaped quantum dots are stacked.

The UV-enhanced catalytic performance of GZQHs with different graphene:ZnO ratios in the composite is shown in Fig. S6. The reaction rate constant is found to increase with increasing graphene:ZnO ratio up to 2:1 and decreases with a further increase in the graphene:ZnO ratio.

The UV–vis spectra of various nitroarenes reduced over GZQHs at different reaction times are shown in Fig. S7. As can be seen in the UV–vis spectra, a reduction in the intensity of a peak around 400nm (3-nitrophenol, 4-nitrophenol) or 450nm (2,4-dinitrophenol) and an increase in the intensity of a peak at 280–300nm indicate the formation of NH2 group from the reduction of NO2 group. The required time for the complete reduction of nitroarenes is listed in Table S1. The required reduction time is found to depend on the position of NO2 group on the benzene ring and the number of NO2 group. The reduction time is 132min, 160min and 190min for 3-nitrophenol, 4-nitrophenol and 2,4-dinitrophenol in the dark condition, respectively and decreases to 18min, 35min and 50min under the UV irradiation, respectively. This result demonstrates that GZQHs are an effective catalyst for the selective reduction of nitroarenes under UV irradiation.

Highly crystalline GZQHs were synthesized via a facile cost-effective chemical-hydrothermal method and their catalytic performance was assessed for the selective reduction of nitroarenes under UV irradiation. The quenched PL intensity of GZQHs compared with GQDs resulted from reduced charge carrier recombination due to photogenerated-electron transfer from ZnO to graphene. As a result, the GZQHs exhibited an excellent catalytic performance for the selective reduction of nitroarenes. The GZQHs are a promising non-metal catalyst to replace expensive noble-metal catalysts that are typically employed in catalytic hydrogenation.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.catcom.2017.08.004.
